response:
# Top 3 changes to reduce risk in `src/billing.ts`

## 1. Break up `generateInvoice` (crime_00001 + crime_00010)
204 lines spanning L50â€“253 â€” 3.4Ă— the domain-function threshold and effectively the reason the whole file is over the 310-line `God File` line (crime_00010). This is the single highest-severity, highest-agent-risk finding in the report (severity 0.9, agent_risk 0.63). Extracting cohesive sections (tax calc, line-item assembly, proration, discount application, persistence) into named helpers does double duty: it kills the `large_function` finding *and* drops the file under the `large_file` threshold without a separate split. It also exposes the responsibilities so the next edit doesn't accidentally collide with code 150 lines away.

## 2. Inject a clock and route all time through it (crime_00004)
7 direct `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls scattered across L57, 63, 92, 198, 238, 250, 273. Every one of those is a hidden coupling to wall time â€” and `src/billing.test.ts` already has weak tests (crime_00006 is zero-assertion, crime_00016 uses weak matchers), so there's no safety net catching temporal bugs today. Threading a `Clock`/`now()` dependency through `generateInvoice` (which #1 makes much easier) means proration windows, due-date math, and audit timestamps can finally be tested with fixed instants instead of monkey-patching `Date`. Pairs naturally with the same fix in `src/date.ts` (crime_00005).

## 3. Encode the refund authorisation rule and rename `calculateRefundPreview` (crime_00008 + crime_00011)
These two findings are about the same code path and reinforce each other:
- L39 carries the rule **"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves"** as a comment â€” prose policy that nearby code doesn't obviously encode (crime_00011). An agent extending refunds will not see it.
- L40â€“44 `calculateRefundPreview` sounds pure but calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` (crime_00008). Anyone â€” human or agent â€” treating it as a pure preview will trigger emails and audit rows.

Fix together: extract the pure calculation as `calculateRefundPreview`, move side effects into a separately-named `commitRefund` (or similar), and encode the owner/enterprise/billing-support rule as a typed guard (e.g. `assertCanRefund(actor, plan)`) so the policy lives in code the type-checker and tests can see. Comment stays only to explain *why*, not *what*.

---

Why this trio over the rest: the remaining billing.ts findings (`String Sprinkles` for `"enterprise"`, 4 TODOs, the file-size flag) are either downstream of #1/#3 or low-leverage cleanup. These three changes together collapse the `God Function`, the `God File`, the temporal coupling, the misleading API, and the hidden authorisation rule â€” i.e. they retire the high-severity finding and four of the five highest-agent-risk findings touching this file.
